Today’s encryption relies on hard math puzzles, like factoring 300-digit numbers.<[silence:40ms]> A quantum computer could crack these instantly using Shor’s algorithm, a 1994 breakthrough that exploits quantum weirdness:<[silence:40ms]> qubits testing all possible answers at once.<[silence:40ms]> Post-quantum cryptography replaces these puzzles with ones even quantum computers can’t shortcut.<[silence:40ms]>

clean_story:
Unbreakable codes. Quantum computers break them.

The term post-quantum cryptography describes encryption built to resist attacks from quantum machines—computers that solve math problems in seconds, not millennia.

Today’s encryption relies on hard math puzzles, like factoring 300-digit numbers. A quantum computer could crack these instantly using Shor’s algorithm, a 1994 breakthrough that exploits quantum weirdness: qubits testing all possible answers at once. Post-quantum cryptography replaces these puzzles with ones even quantum computers can’t shortcut.

One method, lattice-based cryptography, hides data in grids of points so complex that finding patterns would take longer than the universe’s age. Another, hash-based signatures, uses one-time mathematical fingerprints—like sealing a letter with wax that shatters if tampered with.

Governments already test these. In 2022, the U.S. National Institute of Standards chose four post-quantum algorithms for future use. Banks and hospitals will follow, as stolen data encrypted today could be decrypted tomorrow by a quantum thief.

Three related subjects are quantum key distribution, Shor’s algorithm limits, and cryptography in nature.
